title: Chapter 8: The One Who Remembers
content: The knock comes like a heartbeat I wasn't expecting.

I'm still on the kitchen floor — haven't moved since the death penalty notification faded from my phone screen. My legs are asleep. The marble is cold against my thighs. The apartment smells like nothing, because I had the smart diffuser turned off for three days, because the lavender was giving me headaches, because everything gives me headaches now.
Another knock. Three raps. Deliberate.
I freeze. Nobody comes to my door. Not at 4 AM, not ever. My assistant handles deliveries. My manager texts, never visits. My friends—
Wait. Do I have friends?

I pull myself up using the counter, pins and needles screaming through my calves, and shuffle to the door. The security camera feed glows on the panel. A guy in a grey hoodie, hood down, headphones hanging around his neck. He's carrying a plastic bag from the 24-hour Korean place on 32nd — the one with the barely-legitimate health rating and the kimchi that could strip paint. His hair is a disaster, dark and sticking up on one side like he slept on it in a car. He looks like he hasn't slept in two days. He looks like he's been running.
I know him.
My hand is on the deadbolt before my brain catches up. I crack the door an inch. The cold air from the hallway hits my face like a slap.
"Leo?"

The name comes back like muscle memory. Not like remembering — like reflex. The way you'd catch a ball thrown at your face before you consciously register it. I know him. I know his name. I know he's important in some way I can't quite—
"Chloe. Thank God." He exhales like he's been holding his breath since the subway. "Can I come in? It's — can I just—"

I should say no. I should check with someone. I should do a lot of things. But my body is already stepping aside, and he's already moving, and then he's inside my apartment like he's been here before, like he knows where the light switch is, which he shouldn't, but he flicks it on without looking.

The plastic bag crinkles as he sets it on the counter. The smell hits me — fermented chili and garlic and sesame, sharp enough to cut through the stale nothing of the last three days. My stomach growls. I didn't know I was hungry.
"I brought jjajangmyeon," he says, already pulling a laptop from his messenger bag. "And extra kimchi. You always—" He stops himself. Resets. "You used to like the kimchi."
Used to.

He sets up on my kitchen counter like he belongs there. Laptop open, fingers flying across the keyboard before he's even sat down on the barstool. The screen throws blue light across his face, catching the dark circles under his eyes.
"What are you doing here, Leo?"

"I've been tracking data anomalies in Mephisto MCN's network for six months," he says, not looking up. His fingers don't stop moving. Click-clack-click. "Not because of you," he adds, too quickly. "I mean — not directly. Their upload bandwidth is insane for a content-management app. I'm talking petabytes of data flowing out of user devices. That's not cloud sync. That's extraction."
He pulls up a terminal. Green text on black. It looks like the Matrix had a baby with a tax audit.

"I started with the API endpoints. Figured they were scraping behavior data — engagement curves, scroll velocity, pupil dilation if your camera's on. Standard vampiric ad-tech stuff. But the payload sizes didn't match. Way too fat for click data." He finally looks at me. "They're not tracking what you do, Chloe. They're tracking what you feel."
The words sit in the air between us.
"Show me," I say.

He turns the laptop toward me. The screen fills with a visualization he built — custom, I can tell, from the rough edges of the UI. It looks like a heart monitor at first, a scrolling landscape of spiking lines in different colors. But there are more lines than a heart monitor. Way more.

"Neural map," he says. "Or the closest approximation you can build from ambient sensor data. The phone's gyroscope captures micro-tremors. The microphone picks up vocal cord tension. The front camera reads micro-expressions and pupil response. Combined, you get—" He gestures at the screen. "This. Every emotional state you've experienced since you installed the app."

The lines scroll. I watch my own feelings move across the screen in colors I didn't consent to. Blue spikes — sadness, presumably. Green hills — something like contentment. Red peaks — anger, arousal, fear, the algorithm doesn't seem to distinguish. And then—
Flat lines.
Long, flat, empty stretches where the spikes should be. Like someone took an eraser to my emotional history. Like someone went in and smoothed out the peaks.
"What are those?" My voice is steady. I'm impressed by how steady it is.

"The flat parts?" Leo zooms in. "Those are the deductions. See this one?" He highlights a section. "That's two weeks ago. A fourteen-hour window where your grief response just — gone. Not suppressed. Not low. Erased. The data before it shows a spike — your gran, right? You were grieving. And then the app registered the emotional event and—" He makes a poof gesture with his fingers. "Subtracted it. Your phone ate your grief, uploaded it to Mephisto's servers, and left you with that." He points at the flat line. "A gap where a feeling used to be."
The kitchen is very quiet. The refrigerator hums. Somewhere in the building, a pipe groans.

I look at the visualization. I look at the flat sections. They correspond to things, I realize. Big feelings that should be there and aren't. The spikes that got smoothed away. I know I should be horrified. I know I should be terrified. I am those things, technically — I can feel the knowledge of them, the intellectual certainty that this is bad. But the horror itself, the visceral stomach-dropping dread? It's like reading the word "fire" in a textbook instead of being in one.

"Every feeling you've had since you installed the app has been transmitted," Leo says. His voice has shifted. Softer now. "Not just the metrics. The actual emotional data. They're not selling ads, Chloe. They're harvesting."
I stare at the scrolling lines. The gaps stare back.
"You said six months," I say. "How'd you know I was on Mephisto?"
He hesitates. Just a beat. "I — I saw your content. You promoted the partnership. It wasn't hard to figure out."
"Leo."
He doesn't look at me. His jaw tightens. "After you and me—"
"After you and me what?"
The keyboard goes silent. The only sound is the refrigerator, the pipe, his breathing.

He looks up at me. His eyes are red-rimmed from no sleep, and right now they're doing something complicated. Hope flickers first, like a match struck in a dark room. Then hurt — not the sharp kind, the dull kind, the kind that's been carrying. Then confusion, wrinkling his forehead. Then fury, but not at me. At something bigger than both of us.
"Chloe." He says my name like it costs him. "We dated. For two years. You — you don't remember?"
The kitchen tilts slightly. Not physically. But something shifts, like a picture frame knocked crooked on a wall.

I search my memory. I know his name. I know he's important. I can see his face in flashes — laughing at something, walking beside me, a hand on my shoulder. The images are there. The facts are there. Leo Park. Friend since — since when? Since before the followers. Since before any of this. He was there.
But the love — the feeling of being in love with him — it's not there.

I know we were close. I know it in the same way I know that water freezes at thirty-two degrees. It's a fact. Clean, dry, flavorless. A fact without a feeling attached. Like reading my own Wikipedia page.
"I remember you," I say carefully. "I remember — we were close. I know that. But the — the relationship part, the—" I can't find the words. "It's missing."

His face does something I'll replay later, even though I don't feel it the way I should. It collapses, just for a second, the way a building collapses — structural failure, all at once, from the inside. Then he pastes it back together. Badly. The cracks show.

"The app," he says. Not a question. A diagnosis. "It harvested the relationship. The love. That's — that's a high-value emotional state. High bandwidth, sustained signal. Prime target for extraction." He's talking faster now, slipping into jargon like armor. "Romantic attachment would be one of the first things—"
"Leo."
He stops.

I don't know what to say. I'm sorry? I don't feel sorry, not in the way sorry is supposed to feel. Are you okay? That feels like a joke. He just found out the woman he loved doesn't remember loving him, and I'm standing here like I'm reviewing the situation from a conferencing app.
He goes quiet.

Not the noisy kind of quiet where someone's obviously fighting tears. The other kind. The kind that takes up space. He takes off his headphones — the big over-ear ones, worn soft at the pads — and sets them on the counter. He closes his laptop. The blue light dies, and his face goes half-shadow in the kitchen's overhead glow.

I should say something. I should reach for him. I should feel the ache of this moment the way it deserves to be felt. Instead I watch him like I'm watching a movie. A good movie. Something with critical acclaim.
He picks up the plastic bag from the Korean place. Pulls out a small container. Opens it. Pushes it across the counter toward me.
"Eat," he says. "You look like you haven't eaten."

I haven't. I take the container. The kimchi is bright red and glistening, and when I pinch a piece between my fingers and bring it to my mouth, the taste explodes — sour and spicy and alive, more alive than anything I've tasted in weeks. It burns in a way that feels almost like feeling. Almost.
Leo watches me eat one bite. Then he opens his laptop again.
"I'll remember for both of us," he says.

Just like that. No drama. No tremor in his voice. Just a statement, the way someone might say I'll get the door or I'll pay for dinner. He cracks his knuckles — left hand first, then right, each pop precise — and starts typing.

The sound fills the apartment. Click-clack-click-click. Fast but not frantic. Methodical. Like he's done this before, working through pain, like the code is the only place he can put what he's feeling because the real world doesn't have a function for this.
He doesn't cry. He doesn't leave. He works.

I watch him for a while. His hoodie is the soft grey kind that's been washed a thousand times, the fabric pilled at the cuffs. His shoulders are hunched the way people hunch when they're protecting something — their chest, their sternum, the center of them. I notice details the way a camera notices details. That's what I do now. I observe. I don't feel. I record.
He's in pain. I know this the way I know the sky is blue. Factually. From a distance.
"You can stay," I say. "On the couch."
"Thanks. I wasn't asking."
Dry. Sardonic. A flash of the person he must be when he's not sitting in front of the woman who forgot him, watching her not remember what it felt like to be loved by her.
I go back to bed.

The bedroom is cold. The smart thermostat has been on energy-saver mode and I haven't bothered to change it. The sheets are crisp and empty and I slide between them feeling like a very calm placeholder of a person.
I should feel grateful. He's here. He knows things about Mephisto. He can help.
I should feel guilty. He loved me and I don't remember it and he's sitting at my kitchen counter right now, working through that.
I should feel SOMETHING.

I feel interest. Clinical, detached interest. Like I'm watching a nature documentary about an endangered species — the Observed Male, persevering despite habitat loss. I know that's wrong. I know I SHOULD feel more. The knowledge of what I should feel is there. The feeling itself is a flat line.
Across the apartment, the keyboard clacks. The sound is steady. Rhythmic. Like a heartbeat that belongs to someone else.
When did I stop feeling safe around people? When did I stop trusting?

I lie in the dark and count back. The deductions started with Mephisto — that much I know from the data Leo showed me. But the trust thing, the safety thing, that started earlier. When did the world start feeling like glass between me and everyone else?
Answer: at five million followers. I just didn't notice.

The keyboard doesn't stop. He's still working. Fighting something I can't bring myself to fight because the part of me that would fight for people, for connection, for love — that part is sitting on a server somewhere, being bought and sold by something that wears a corporate logo like a mask.

Leo is here. He knows things. He can help. But I can't feel what I should feel for him, and the cold air and the warm kimchi and the sound of his keyboard are all happening on the other side of a wall I can't see, and I don't know if that makes him more dangerous or less.
I close my eyes.
He doesn't stop typing.
